content:
4 min read
Just now

--

Part 2 of the “Concurrency & Parallelism Explained” series. counter += 1 looks atomic. It isn't. That gap is where the bug lives.
You write a simple multithreaded counter: ten threads, each incrementing a shared counter a hundred thousand times. You expect the final value to be one million. You run it, and get 743,219. Run it again: 681,044. A different wrong answer every time, and no exception, no crash — just silently incorrect results.
Welcome to the race condition, the defining bug of concurrent programming. It’s invisible in single-threaded code, impossible to reproduce reliably, and rooted in a truth most developers never think about: the simple-looking operations in your code aren’t actually indivisible. Let me show exactly where the bug hides and how locks fix it.
counter += 1 isn't one step — it's three: read, add, write. A race condition is two threads interleaving those steps and stepping on each other in the gap. The bug isn't your logic; it's the assumption that one line means one operation.
Where the Bug Actually Is
That innocent counter += 1 compiles to roughly three separate machine operations:
1. READ counter into a register (thread sees 100)
2. ADD 1 to the register (register = 101)
3. WRITE the register to counter (counter = 101)Now imagine two threads run it at the “same time” and interleave:
Thread A: reads counter = 100
Thread B: reads counter = 100 ← before A wrote back!
Thread A: computes 101, writes 101
Thread B: computes 101, writes 101 ← overwrites A; one increment LOSTBoth threads incremented, but the counter only went up by one. That’s the lost update, multiplied across millions of operations — which is exactly why you got 743,219 instead of a million. The data wasn’t protected during the read-modify-write, so threads clobbered each other in the gaps.
The Fix: Mutual Exclusion (a Lock)
A lock (mutex) ensures only one thread can be in a critical section at a time. The others wait their turn:
import threading
counter = 0
lock = threading.Lock()def worker():
global counter
for _ in range(100000):
with lock: # only ONE thread can hold the lock at a time
counter += 1 # read-modify-write is now indivisibleNow the three steps (read, add, write) happen as an uninterruptible unit, because no other thread can enter the locked section until the current one leaves. Run it and you get exactly one million, every time. The lock turned “three steps anyone can interrupt” into “one step nobody can.”
Fix #1: Keep Critical Sections Small
Locks have a cost: while one thread holds the lock, others wait, which destroys the parallelism you wanted. So lock the smallest necessary region:
# BAD: holds the lock during slow work — other threads stall
with lock:
data = fetch_from_network() # slow! everyone waits for this
counter += 1# GOOD: do slow work outside, lock only the shared mutation
data = fetch_from_network() # no lock held
with lock:
counter += 1 # lock held for microsecondsThe rule: hold locks for as short a time as possible, and never do I/O or slow work while holding one. A coarse lock around everything is correct but serializes your whole program.
Fix #2: Prefer Higher-Level Tools Over Raw Locks
Raw locks are easy to misuse (forget to release, lock in the wrong order — that’s Part 3’s deadlocks). Reach for safer abstractions when you can:

• Atomic operations — many languages offer atomic integers/counters that do read-modify-write as a single hardware instruction, no explicit lock needed.

• Concurrent data structures — thread-safe queues, maps, etc. that handle locking internally.

• Message passing — instead of sharing memory and locking it, have threads communicate by sending messages (a thread-safe Queue), so there's no shared state to protect. ("Don't communicate by sharing memory; share memory by communicating.")

• Immutability — data that never changes can’t have a race. Share read-only data freely.
The best way to avoid race conditions is often to avoid shared mutable state in the first place.
Fix #3: Know That You Can’t Test Races Away
Race conditions are non-deterministic — they depend on exact timing, so they may appear once in ten thousand runs and never in your test suite. You cannot reliably reproduce or test them into existence. The defense is design, not testing:

• Identify every piece of shared mutable state.

• Protect each one deliberately (lock, atomic, or eliminate the sharing).

• Assume any unprotected shared write will eventually race in production.
This is why concurrency bugs are so feared: passing tests prove nothing. Correctness comes from reasoning about the shared state up front.
Putting It Together

• The bug: “atomic-looking” operations are multi-step; threads interleave in the gaps.

• The fix: a lock makes the critical section indivisible.

• Keep locks small — never do slow work or I/O while holding one.

• Prefer atomics, concurrent structures, message passing, or immutability — avoid shared mutable state when you can.
The Takeaway
Race conditions feel like dark magic until you see the one thing causing them: the operations you treat as single steps are really several, and concurrent threads slip into the gaps and corrupt shared data. A lock fixes it by making the critical section indivisible — but locks must be small and used carefully, and the deeper fix is usually to avoid shared mutable state altogether through atomics, message passing, or immutability. Above all, remember these bugs are non-deterministic: you can’t test them away, so you must design for them by protecting every shared write deliberately. Respect the gap between “one line” and “one operation,” and concurrency stops producing mysterious wrong answers.

This is Part 2 of the Concurrency & Parallelism Explained series. Next up: deadlocks — what happens when your locks start waiting on each other forever.
What’s the most baffling non-deterministic bug you’ve ever chased? 👇
